original_abstract: Quantifying the semantic and structural similarity between words, sentences, and entire documents is a fundamental challenge in natural language processing. It serves as a cornerstone for numerous applications, including information retrieval, document clustering, word-sense disambiguation, machine translation, and automated grading systems. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the field by categorizing existing methodologies into three primary frameworks: string-based, corpus-based, and knowledge-based approaches. Additionally, we examine hybrid models that integrate these distinct techniques to achieve more robust performance across diverse linguistic tasks.
humanized_abstract: Words, sentences and documents are essential building blocks of natural language and understanding their semantic and structural similarity is crucial for a wide range of Natural Language Processing tasks. Several algorithms have been proposed for finding the similarity between words, sentences and documents that has become a cornerstone of information retrieval, document clustering, word sense disambiguation, machine translation, and automatic grading, among other applications. This survey presents a summary of the state of the art techniques and models by segregating them into three broad categories: string-based, corpus-based and knowledge-based approaches. Hybrid approaches combining different models and techniques are also discussed in detail, focusing on their applicability and strengths in various domains and applications.
